title: Certified Kubernetes Administrator (CKA)
note: The real CKA is a hands-on, performance-based exam (live cluster tasks), not multiple choice. These MCQ sets build conceptual recall and command knowledge — pair them with real kubectl practice on a cluster.

questions.stem: Which component stores all cluster state as the source of truth?

options.explanation: Correct. etcd is the consistent key-value store backing cluster state.
Wrong. The scheduler places pods; it stores nothing persistently.
Wrong. The kubelet runs pods on nodes.
Wrong. kube-proxy programs service networking.

questions.stem: Which component schedules pods onto nodes?

options.explanation: Correct. The scheduler assigns pods to suitable nodes.
Wrong. The kubelet runs assigned pods, it does not schedule them.
Wrong. etcd stores state.
Wrong. kube-proxy handles service traffic.

questions.stem: Which agent runs on each node and manages the pods there?

options.explanation: Correct. The kubelet ensures containers described in PodSpecs run.
Wrong. The API server is the control-plane front end.
Wrong. It runs control loops, not node pods.
Wrong. etcd stores state.

questions.stem: Which control-plane component exposes the Kubernetes API?

options.explanation: Correct. The API server is the front end for the control plane.
Wrong. It only schedules pods.
Wrong. The kubelet is a node agent.
Wrong. It handles service networking.

questions.stem: Which tool bootstraps a conformant cluster control plane and joins nodes?

options.explanation: Correct. kubeadm initializes the control plane and joins workers.
Wrong. kubectl is the API client, not a bootstrapper.
Wrong. The kubelet is the node agent.
Wrong. etcdctl manages etcd directly.

questions.stem: To back up cluster state, what should you snapshot?

options.explanation: Correct. Backing up etcd captures all cluster objects.
Wrong. That does not contain cluster object state.
Wrong. Those are derived, not the source of truth.
Wrong. The CNI is networking, not state storage.

questions.stem: Which object manages a stateless replicated set of pods with rolling updates?

options.explanation: Correct. Deployments manage ReplicaSets and rolling updates.
Wrong. DaemonSets run one pod per node.
Wrong. StatefulSets are for stable identity/storage.
Wrong. Jobs run to completion.

questions.stem: Which object ensures a copy of a pod runs on every (matching) node?

options.explanation: Correct. DaemonSets place one pod per eligible node.
Wrong. Deployments do not guarantee one-per-node.
Wrong. Jobs run to completion.
Wrong. ReplicaSets keep a count, not per-node placement.

questions.stem: Which object provides stable network identity and ordered, persistent storage?

options.explanation: Correct. StatefulSets give stable identities and per-pod volumes.
Wrong. Deployment pods are interchangeable.
Wrong. DaemonSets are per-node, not identity-stable sets.
Wrong. Jobs are run-to-completion.

questions.stem: Which mechanism repels pods from a node unless they tolerate it?

options.explanation: Correct. Taints repel pods lacking a matching toleration.
Wrong. nodeSelector attracts; it is set on the pod.
Wrong. Limits cap usage, not placement by condition.
Wrong. PDBs limit voluntary disruptions.

questions.stem: Which pod field requests that a pod land only on nodes with a given label?

options.explanation: Correct. nodeSelector constrains scheduling to labeled nodes.
Wrong. Taints are on nodes and repel pods.
Wrong. Tolerations allow scheduling onto tainted nodes.
Wrong. Limits cap usage, not target labels.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions prepare a node for maintenance safely?

options.explanation: Correct. cordon marks the node unschedulable.
Correct. drain evicts pods (respecting PDBs), skipping DaemonSets.
Wrong. Deleting removes the node object entirely.
Wrong. PreferNoSchedule is a soft hint, not a guarantee.

questions.stem: What does a readiness probe control?

options.text: Whether a pod receives traffic from Services
options.explanation: Correct. Failing readiness removes the pod from Service endpoints.
Wrong. That is the liveness probe.
Wrong. Probes run after scheduling.
Wrong. Probes do not control image pulls.

questions.stem: What does a liveness probe failure cause?

options.explanation: Correct. Liveness failures trigger a container restart.

options.text: The pod is removed from Service endpoints
options.explanation: Wrong. That is readiness.
Wrong. Probes do not cordon nodes.
Wrong. Probes do not scale workloads.

questions.stem: Which Service type exposes a pod on a static port across every node's IP?

options.explanation: Correct. NodePort opens the same port on all nodes.
Wrong. ClusterIP is internal-only.
Wrong. It maps to a DNS name via CNAME.
Wrong. Headless returns pod IPs directly.

questions.stem: Which Service type is the default and internal-only?

options.explanation: Correct. ClusterIP exposes the Service within the cluster only.
Wrong. NodePort exposes on nodes externally.
Wrong. LoadBalancer provisions an external LB.
Wrong. It is a DNS alias.

questions.stem: Which object routes external HTTP(S) traffic to Services by host/path rules?

options.explanation: Correct. Ingress provides L7 host/path routing to Services.

Wrong. PVs are storage, not routing.

questions.stem: What does a NetworkPolicy control?

options.explanation: Correct. NetworkPolicies restrict pod-to-pod traffic.
Wrong. That is the scheduler/affinity.
Wrong. Storage is unrelated to NetworkPolicy.
Wrong. That is liveness probes.

questions.stem: Which add-on provides pod networking (the CNI's role)?

options.explanation: Correct. CoreDNS resolves Service and pod DNS names.
Wrong. The scheduler does not do DNS.

questions.stem: What does a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) do?

options.text: Requests storage that binds to a PersistentVolume
options.explanation: Correct. A PVC is a user's request for storage.
Wrong. Images are set in the pod spec.
Wrong. That is a Service/Ingress.
Wrong. That is the scheduler.

questions.stem: A PVC stays Pending. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: No PV or StorageClass satisfies its size/access mode
options.explanation: Correct. Without a matching PV or dynamic provisioner the PVC stays Pending.
Wrong. Replica count does not affect binding.

Wrong. kube-proxy is service networking.

questions.stem: What does a StorageClass enable?

options.text: Dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumes on demand
options.explanation: Correct. StorageClasses let PVs be created automatically.
Wrong. Storage classes are not networking.
Wrong. That is affinity/taints.
Wrong. Unrelated to storage classes.

questions.stem: Which access mode allows a volume to be mounted read-write by a single node?

options.explanation: Correct. RWO mounts read-write by one node.
Wrong. ROX is read-only by many nodes.
Wrong. RWX is read-write by many nodes.
Wrong. No such access mode.

options.explanation: Correct. Retain preserves the volume and data after the claim is gone.
Wrong. That is the Delete policy.

Wrong. Reclaim policy is about the volume, not pods.

questions.stem: A pod shows ImagePullBackOff. What is the likely cause?

questions.stem: What does kubectl apply --dry-run=client do?

questions.stem: node02 went NotReady after a kernel update. SSH works. Your first two commands on the node?

questions.stem: A pod stays Pending; describe shows 'node(s) had untolerated taint {dedicated: gpu}'. You DO want it on those nodes. Fix?

options.explanation: Correct. `tolerations: [{key: dedicated, operator: Equal, value: gpu, effect: NoSchedule}]` lets it schedule there.

questions.stem: Before a risky control-plane change you must back up etcd on a kubeadm cluster. Command shape?

options.explanation: Correct. `ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd.db --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=…server.crt --key=…server.key`.

questions.stem: etcd is corrupted; you have last night's snapshot. Key restore step on the kubeadm control plane?

options.explanation: Correct. `etcdctl snapshot restore /backup/etcd.db --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restore`, then edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml's hostPath.

questions.stem: Upgrading a kubeadm cluster from 1.31 to 1.32. Correct order of operations?

options.explanation: Correct. `apt install kubeadm=1.32.x`, `kubeadm upgrade plan && kubeadm upgrade apply v1.32.x`, then per-node drain → upgrade kubelet → uncordon.

questions.stem: Node maintenance: pods must leave node03 gracefully, DaemonSets stay, and emptyDir scratch data is acceptable to lose. Command?

questions.stem: Drain hangs forever: 'Cannot evict pod as it would violate the pod's disruption budget.' Healthy approach?

questions.stem: A Service has endpoints '<none>' though its pods run fine. First thing to compare?

questions.stem: A distroless container has no shell, and you must inspect its filesystem and processes live. Tool?

questions.stem: A bad image tag shipped to prod via Deployment 'web'. Fastest rollback?

questions.stem: You need a join command for a new worker, but the original bootstrap token expired. Command?

questions.stem: A teammate's kubectl talks to staging when they think it's prod. The two commands to diagnose and fix?

options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list --resource=pods -n ci` + `kubectl create rolebinding ci-read --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=ci:builder -n ci`.

questions.stem: Two pods on different nodes can't reach each other; same-node pods can. Pings to node IPs work. Prime suspect?

Wrong. 'default' is not renameable.
Wrong. Cost explosion to avoid a flag.

questions.stem: A vendor needs read-only access to exactly one namespace for a week. Cleanest grant?

options.text: Bind the built-in 'view' ClusterRole via a RoleBinding in that namespace
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl create rolebinding vendor-view --clusterrole=view --user=vendor@ext -n project-x` — ClusterRole reused, scope stays namespaced.
Wrong. That grants read on every namespace.
Wrong. Reinventing the built-in.
Wrong. Full your-level access, no audit identity.

questions.stem: An in-cluster app must call the API as itself with minimal blast radius. Pod spec essentials?

options.text: A dedicated ServiceAccount named in serviceAccountName, bound to a minimal Role
options.explanation: Correct. Per-app SAs + least-privilege Roles keep tokens scoped; default SA sharing is the anti-pattern.
Wrong. Human creds in pods.

options.text: Use the default SA with cluster-admin
options.explanation: Wrong. Maximum blast radius.

options.text: Hardcode a long-lived token in env
options.explanation: Wrong. Static secrets where projected tokens exist.

questions.stem: An internal-only Service must move to external exposure through the cloud LB, TLS at the edge, minimal object churn. Change?

options.text: Patch the Service type to LoadBalancer (or front it with Ingress for L7/TLS)
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl patch svc web -p '{"spec":{"type":"LoadBalancer"}}'`; choose Ingress when host/path/TLS management belongs in-cluster.

options.text: Recreate everything in a public namespace
options.explanation: Wrong. Namespaces aren't network boundaries.
Wrong. Port conflicts and node coupling.
Wrong. port-forward is a debug tool tied to your session.

questions.stem: You need to hit a ClusterIP service from your laptop for five minutes of debugging, no infra changes. Tool?

options.explanation: Correct. Temporary tunnel through the API server — vanishes when you Ctrl-C.

options.text: Create a NodePort and delete it later
options.explanation: Wrong. Real (forgettable) infra change for a 5-minute need.
Wrong. Heavyweight and risky.

options.text: ssh tunnel to a node IP
options.explanation: Wrong. Works sometimes; port-forward is the purpose-built path.

questions.stem: Pods must resolve 'legacy.corp.internal' which only the corporate DNS knows. Cluster-level fix?

options.text: Add a forward zone for corp.internal in the CoreDNS Corefile (ConfigMap) pointing at corporate resolvers
options.explanation: Correct. `forward corp.internal 10.10.0.2` in the Corefile; CoreDNS reloads and pods resolve transparently.
Wrong. Static IP pinning duplicated across workloads.
Wrong. Pod DNS flows through CoreDNS, and node edits don't survive reimages.
Wrong. Per-record objects for an entire zone.

questions.stem: Compliance: 'payments' pods may talk to 'db' pods on 5432 and nothing else, in or out. Skeleton?

options.text: Default-deny ingress+egress NetworkPolicy in the namespace, plus a policy allowing payments→db:5432 (and DNS egress)
options.explanation: Correct. Deny-all via empty podSelector with both policyTypes, then explicit allows — remember UDP/TCP 53 to kube-dns or name resolution dies.

options.text: One allow rule, no default deny
options.explanation: Wrong. Without deny-all, everything else still flows.
Wrong. Node firewalls can't see pod identity.
Wrong. Policy does this in-cluster.

questions.stem: An app writes scratch data two containers in the same pod must share; it can vanish on pod restart. Volume?

options.explanation: Correct. Pod-lifetime scratch space shared by all containers — add `medium: Memory` for tmpfs speed if needed.
Wrong. Persistence wasn't wanted; PVCs add provisioning weight.
Wrong. Leaks data to the node and breaks portability.
Wrong. Read-mostly config, ~1Mi limits.

questions.stem: Each replica of a StatefulSet needs its own disk that follows it across rescheduling. Mechanism?

options.explanation: Correct. Templates mint a PVC per replica (data-db-0, data-db-1…) that rebinds to the same pod identity.
Wrong. RWO can't multi-attach, and shared state defeats per-replica disks.
Wrong. Dies with the pod.
Wrong. Templates automate exactly this.

questions.stem: You must confirm which StorageClass is cluster default and change it to 'fast-ssd'. Commands?

options.text: kubectl get sc, then patch the is-default-class annotation on both classes
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl patch storageclass fast-ssd -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'` (and set the old one to "false").

options.text: Edit every PVC to name the class
options.explanation: Wrong. Default exists so PVCs can omit it.
Wrong. Existing PVs may reference it.
Wrong. The annotation, not the name, confers defaultness.

questions.stem: Listing events for one flapping pod, newest last, without the rest of the namespace noise. Command?

options.text: kubectl events --for pod/api-7f9c --watch (or kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=api-7f9c)
options.explanation: Correct. Scoped events give the pod's own timeline; --watch streams new ones.
Wrong. Cluster-wide haystack.
Wrong. App output, not lifecycle events.
Wrong. Wrong object.

questions.stem: A revamped checkout must deploy with the old one still serving, switchable instantly and revertibly (blue/green). With core objects, the switch is…

options.text: Repoint the Service selector from version: blue to version: green
options.explanation: Correct. Both Deployments run; flipping the selector label moves 100% of traffic atomically, and back.
Wrong. That's for rolling updates within one Deployment.
Wrong. The whole point is keeping the fallback warm.
Wrong. Services don't follow across namespaces.

questions.stem: kubeadm cluster: API server cert expires next week (cluster is a year old). Renewal?

options.text: kubeadm certs check-expiration, then kubeadm certs renew all and restart control-plane components
options.explanation: Correct. kubeadm manages cert rotation: check, `kubeadm certs renew all`, then bounce the static pods (move manifests out/in or restart kubelet).
Wrong. Renewal is built-in.
Wrong. kubeadm renews on upgrade, but a non-upgraded year-old cluster hits expiry.

options.text: openssl by hand against etcd CA
options.explanation: Wrong. Possible but error-prone; kubeadm wraps it.

questions.stem: After cert renewal your kubectl gets 'x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'. Local fix?

options.text: Refresh your kubeconfig from the cluster (e.g. re-copy /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf)
options.explanation: Correct. The client still trusts/presents old data; the regenerated admin.conf carries the current certs.
Wrong. Permanent MITM exposure to dodge a config refresh.
Wrong. The binary doesn't hold certs.
Wrong. Server side is fine; the client config is stale.

questions.stem: Critical system pods must survive node pressure while batch pods get evicted first. Mechanism?

options.text: PriorityClasses: high value for critical, low for batch
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl create priorityclass critical --value=100000` + priorityClassName in specs; eviction and preemption respect priority.
Wrong. All replicas remain equally evictable.
Wrong. Restart ≠ eviction protection.
Wrong. Networking mode, irrelevant.

questions.stem: Replicas must spread evenly across three zones, tolerating at most one zone of imbalance. Spec?

options.explanation: Correct. Spread constraints generalize anti-affinity: even zone distribution with bounded skew.
Wrong. That separates by node, not balanced-by-zone.
Wrong. Manual sharding the scheduler can do.
Wrong. Replicas share one template.

questions.stem: You suspect a node's container runtime, not Kubernetes, is the problem. Node-local tool to list/inspect containers?

options.text: crictl ps / crictl logs on the node
options.explanation: Correct. crictl speaks CRI directly to containerd/CRI-O — the right layer when kubelet's view is in doubt.
Wrong. Most modern nodes have no Docker daemon.
Wrong. That's the API view you're trying to bypass.
Wrong. etcd isn't the node runtime.

questions.stem: A pod needs read-only access to node log files at /var/log for a node-debug DaemonSet. Volume type and caveat?

options.text: hostPath, used sparingly and read-only — it couples pods to nodes and raises security flags
options.explanation: Correct. hostPath is legitimate for node agents; mount readOnly: true and restrict via admission policy.
Wrong. PVs abstract away the node — opposite of the need.
Wrong. That's fresh scratch, not the node's logs.
Wrong. Wrong direction entirely.

questions.stem: You need every API resource kind the cluster supports that is namespaced, to script a backup. Command?

options.explanation: Correct. Lists kinds with their shortnames and API groups; add -o name for scripting.
Wrong. 'all' is a curated subset, famously incomplete.
Wrong. CRDs make every cluster different.
Wrong. Raw keys, not a kind catalog.

questions.stem: An external database at 203.0.113.40:5432 should be reachable by pods via the cluster-DNS name 'db'. No proxying wanted, just naming. Object?

options.text: A Service without selector plus a manual EndpointSlice (or ExternalName for a DNS name)
options.explanation: Correct. Selector-less Service + endpoints maps a stable cluster name to external IPs; ExternalName covers the CNAME case.
Wrong. Filtering, not naming.
Wrong. Inbound L7, not outbound naming.
Wrong. Per-pod duplication.

questions.stem: Saving a snapshot of all YAML in namespace 'prod' before a risky change. One-liner (accepting its limits)?

options.text: kubectl get all,cm,secret,pvc,ing -n prod -o yaml > prod-backup.yaml
options.explanation: Correct. A pragmatic manifest snapshot — remembering 'all' omits some kinds, hence listing extras explicitly.

options.text: kubectl get all -n prod (table output) > file
options.explanation: Wrong. Tables aren't restorable.

options.text: Trust etcd backups alone for app-level rollback
options.explanation: Wrong. etcd restore is cluster-wide surgery, not per-namespace rollback.
Wrong. No.

questions.stem: Devs ask why their pod 'disappeared' — it completed. The object semantics they hit?

options.text: It was a Job pod (or restartPolicy Never/OnFailure) that ran to completion — Completed pods linger until cleaned
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Succeeded` shows them; ttlSecondsAfterFinished tidies Jobs.

options.text: Kubernetes deletes random pods under load
options.explanation: Wrong. Eviction is principled, and this was completion.
Wrong. Occam disagrees.
Wrong. That's a visible failed state, not disappearance.

questions.stem: Service type LoadBalancer is Pending forever on a bare-metal cluster. Why, and the standard fix?

options.text: No cloud controller exists to provision an LB; install something like MetalLB to serve LoadBalancer IPs
options.explanation: Correct. type: LoadBalancer is fulfilled by a provider integration — bare metal needs MetalLB (or similar) to allocate addresses.
Wrong. It's valid; nothing implements it.
Wrong. proxying isn't LB provisioning.
Wrong. Nothing is coming.

questions.stem: Secrets in etcd must not be readable plaintext if the etcd disk leaks. Cluster-level control?

options.text: Enable encryption at rest via an EncryptionConfiguration on the API server
options.explanation: Correct. An encryption provider config (aescbc/kms) makes the API server encrypt Secrets before they touch etcd.
Wrong. base64 is encoding, not encryption.
Wrong. RBAC guards the API, not the disk.
Wrong. Traffic filtering, not at-rest crypto.

questions.stem: The control plane must reject pods running as root in namespace 'restricted', cluster-natively. Mechanism?

options.text: Pod Security Admission: label the namespace pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
options.explanation: Correct. PSA's 'restricted' profile blocks root, privilege escalation, and more at admission.
Wrong. PSP was removed in 1.25.
Wrong. Network filtering can't see UIDs.
Wrong. Admission control is the enforcement layer.

questions.stem: A container needs exactly: no privilege escalation, read-only root FS, run as UID 1000. Where do these live?

options.text: securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, runAsUser: 1000
options.explanation: Correct. Container-level securityContext carries all three flags.
Wrong. Annotations are inert metadata.
Wrong. Resources, not security flags.
Wrong. USER helps, but runtime enforcement belongs in the spec.

questions.stem: Node 'worker-3' shows DiskPressure and evictions began. After SSH, where did the space typically go on a container node?

options.text: Container images/logs: crictl rmi --prune dead images, check /var/log and emptyDir-heavy pods
options.explanation: Correct. Image bloat and runaway logs dominate; kubelet GC thresholds may also need tuning.
Wrong. Workers don't run etcd.
Wrong. Kilobytes.
Wrong. Memory, not disk.

questions.stem: A pod must be reachable by other pods only at its individual identity (no load balancing), e.g. Kafka brokers. Object combo?

options.text: StatefulSet + headless Service giving kafka-0.kafka.ns.svc DNS per pod
options.explanation: Correct. Headless Services publish per-pod records keyed to StatefulSet ordinals — exactly the broker-addressing model.
Wrong. ClusterIP load-balances; identities blur.
Wrong. Node coupling and port sprawl.
Wrong. Identity by node, not by pod ordinal.

questions.stem: You want declarative, reviewable changes: all kubectl mutations must flow through files in Git. Apply discipline?

options.text: kubectl apply -f applied from version-controlled manifests (or kustomize -k), never imperative edits
options.explanation: Correct. apply's three-way merge supports the GitOps loop; `kubectl diff -f` previews changes.

options.text: kubectl edit in emergencies becomes the norm
options.explanation: Wrong. Live edits drift from Git instantly.
Wrong. create can't reconcile updates.

options.text: Patch via curl to the API
options.explanation: Wrong. Bypasses the whole review trail.

questions.stem: Two environments need the same manifests with different replica counts and image tags, no templating engine. Built-in kubectl mechanism?

options.explanation: Correct. Base + overlays patch fields per env, native in kubectl.
Wrong. Stringly-typed templating with no validation.
Wrong. The drift machine.
Wrong. Config management is orthogonal.

questions.stem: kubectl apply fails: 'field is immutable' on a Deployment's selector. Reality and path forward?

options.text: Selectors are immutable; create the Deployment under a new name (or delete and recreate) with the corrected selector
options.explanation: Correct. label-selector immutability protects ReplicaSet ownership — plan label schemas before first apply.
Wrong. That's for server-side apply field conflicts, not immutables.
Wrong. Bypassing validation breaks ownership math.
Wrong. The rule is by design.

questions.stem: Your Ingress needs TLS for shop.example.com. Cert and key exist as files. Wiring?

options.text: kubectl create secret tls shop-tls --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key, then reference it under spec.tls in the Ingress
options.explanation: Correct. TLS secrets + the Ingress tls block let the controller terminate HTTPS for the host.

options.text: Paste the PEM into the Ingress YAML
options.explanation: Wrong. Ingress references secrets; it doesn't embed keys.
Wrong. Private keys belong in Secrets.

options.text: Terminate TLS in every app pod
options.explanation: Wrong. Possible, but the question wires the Ingress.

questions.stem: kubectl scale must react to real traffic instead of being run by humans at 9 AM daily. Object?

options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl autoscale deployment web --min=3 --max=20 --cpu-percent=70` automates what the humans were doing.
Wrong. Time-based guessing, not load-based reaction.
Wrong. Rollout pacing, not steady-state scaling.
Wrong. Node capacity ≠ pod count policy.

questions.stem: A namespace delete hangs in 'Terminating' for hours. Root cause class to investigate?

options.text: Resources with finalizers (often from uninstalled operators/APIs) blocking namespace finalization
options.explanation: Correct. `kubectl get ns stuck -o json | jq .spec.finalizers` and `kubectl api-resources --verbs=list -n stuck` reveal the holdouts.
Wrong. Compaction is storage hygiene, not delete-blocking.

options.text: Too many pods to delete quickly
options.explanation: Wrong. Pod deletion is fast; finalizers are the classic hang.
Wrong. Denial errors immediately; it doesn't hang.

questions.stem: For the CKA-style task 'create a pod with image nginx in namespace web, fast', the speed move is…

options.explanation: Correct. Imperative commands are exam-speed; add --dry-run=client -o yaml when you need the manifest.
Wrong. Slower and typo-prone under the clock.
Wrong. Tooling overkill for one pod.
Wrong. That makes a Deployment, not the asked-for pod.

questions.stem: An NFS share must back several pods read-write simultaneously across nodes. PV essentials?

options.text: A PV with accessModes ReadWriteMany (NFS supports it), claimed by a RWX PVC
options.explanation: Correct. RWX is the multi-node read-write mode; block-storage CSI drivers typically can't, NFS/CephFS can.
Wrong. RWO mounts on one node.
Wrong. They need writes.
Wrong. Independent scratch spaces, not a share.

questions.stem: API latency spiked cluster-wide; control-plane node disk shows etcd fsync warnings in logs. Reading?

options.text: etcd is disk-latency sensitive — slow fsync starves the whole API; move etcd to fast SSD/dedicated disk
options.explanation: Correct. 'etcdserver: ... took too long' warnings mean storage can't keep up; etcd I/O is the control plane's heartbeat.
Wrong. Workers don't relieve etcd I/O.
Wrong. DNS isn't API latency.
Wrong. More client pressure, worse outcome.

questions.stem: You need pods rescheduled off a node automatically if it stays NotReady. Who does that, and roughly when?

options.text: The node controller evicts after the eviction timeout (~5m default); controllers then recreate pods elsewhere
options.explanation: Correct. Taint-based eviction (node.kubernetes.io/unreachable) removes pods so Deployments/StatefulSets reschedule them.
Wrong. Service plumbing only.
Wrong. Manual drain is for planned maintenance; failures auto-evict.
Wrong. Storage doesn't schedule.

questions.stem: Pods resolve external names slowly; you find ndots:5 causing search-domain storms. Pod-level tuning?

options.text: dnsConfig options to lower ndots (e.g. ndots: 2) for external-heavy workloads
options.explanation: Correct. spec.dnsConfig lets you tune resolv.conf options per pod, cutting wasted search-suffix lookups.
Wrong. Sledgehammer with side effects.
Wrong. Capacity won't fix per-query multiplication.
Wrong. You can't enumerate the internet.

questions.stem: You must drain and patch all 12 workers tonight with max 2 down at any time, app availability protected. Combination?

options.text: PodDisruptionBudgets on critical apps + scripted serial/2-at-a-time kubectl drain/uncordon
options.explanation: Correct. PDBs make drain refuse to break availability; the loop (`for n in …; do kubectl drain $n …; patch; kubectl uncordon $n; done`) paces the fleet.
Wrong. Total outage.
Wrong. Ungraceful kills and PDBs bypassed.
Wrong. Identity churn for a patch.

questions.stem: A pod must consume 14 ConfigMap keys as env vars without 14 env entries. Spec shortcut?

options.explanation: Correct. envFrom imports every key of the ConfigMap as env vars in one stanza.
Wrong. Works, verbosely — envFrom is the shortcut asked for.

options.text: Mount and parse a file in the entrypoint
options.explanation: Wrong. App changes for a spec feature.
Wrong. Sensitivity wasn't the issue.

questions.stem: 'kubectl top' returns 'Metrics API not available'. Dependency to install/verify?

options.explanation: Correct. top, and HPA's resource metrics, ride the Metrics API served by metrics-server.
Wrong. Great, but kubectl top doesn't read Prometheus.
Wrong. Deprecated years ago.
Wrong. UI, not the metrics pipeline.

questions.stem: A deploy must pause automatically if the new version crashes, instead of marching on. Which TWO Deployment behaviors/fields are doing the protective work?

options.text: Rolling update halts when new pods never become Ready (readiness gates progress)
options.explanation: Correct. Unready new pods stop the rollout from advancing past maxUnavailable.

options.text: progressDeadlineSeconds marks the rollout failed for visibility/automation
options.explanation: Correct. The Progressing condition flips to False after the deadline — your CD can auto-rollback on it.
Wrong. That's a pod/Job field.
Wrong. Shutdown timing, not rollout safety.
